original: can of Twilit
a canned, alcoholic beverage promising urban delights

Twilit is a genemod packaged as if it were an ordinary, everyday alcoholic beverage. The cocktail’s can is decorated with a purple-hued, neon-lit cityscape. Your codex warns you that while it is slightly grape flavored and just intoxicating enough to have a good time, its intended use is to make the consumer more like the natives of the world it hails from, cock-tail and all.

The bubbly carbonation lingers on your tongue, sizzling with a rather enjoyable warmth. The intoxicating spirit has left you a little light-headed, making your vision a softer blend of colors and less-distinct shapes. It’s as if the whole world’s just a little more supple and a little less rigid. You weave your fingers in front of your eyes, enjoying the slight disconnect of the afterimages they trace through the air. Wait... just how alcoholic was that cocktail? You screw up your face in concentration and put your hands on your hips in thought. Oh! Your hips are a little... wider? No, it doesn’t seem right. You shake your head and run a hand over your face, as if to brush away the fuzziness. Maybe you’re just drunk, but something doesn’t feel quite right. You pull out your codex and turn it on mirror mode. Your face... is it softer? Smoother? Your cheeks look a little fuller. Your chin, a little rounder. You blink several times. It’s definitely not too different, but there’s something. Maybe a little more feminine than before?
Twilit

You crack open the cold one, enjoying the sudden onset of cold and the bubbling hiss of carbonation. There’s something just so appealing about that. Tossing the alcoholic cola back, you can’t help but enjoy the sweet rush of your blueberry- scratch that, PURPLE - drink. A pleasant warmth rises in your brain as the alcohol rushes through your system and before long, you’ve polished off the cocktail and heave a sigh of contented delight. One’s never exactly sure what kind of changes these genemods will bring, but at least you can get a little buzzed off of this one.

You turn the can of zaika soda around, admiring the neon-lit cityscape printed on its deep purple exterior. Deeply silhouetted skyscrapers rise up in a perpetual gloom, cut through by eye-catching streaks of sizzling pinks and blues. It’s the sort of panoramic that gives the planet-spanning belt-city a kind of darkly majestic mystery. However, you can’t help but note the total lack of warning labels on the alcoholic transformative. The aesthetics of the beverage really are on point. 

With a degree of trepidation, you crack open the can’s tabbed peak and hear the sizzling hiss of the carbonated drink. Though it was cool to the touch before, exposing the fluid to the air has caused some kind of reaction, chilling the drink considerably in mere moments. That’s... odd. Well, you suppose Bright Water is nothing, if not dedicated to their art.

Clenching your eyes shut, you put the drink to your lips and throw your head back. The fizzing foam of your libation tickles your sinuses on the way down, distracting you slightly from the first taste. Like any cocktail, Twilit is a blend of flavors, each complementing and suppressing the others. There’s the faintest hint of bitterness, like you’d expect out of a beer; a tingling kick, normally reserved for liquor; and a sweet cascade of something almost but not quite entirely unlike real blueberries. If anything, it tastes like... purple. Weirdly appropriate, you suppose, but still. The soda pop is...

It’s actually not bad. The cold keeps the sweetness from being too cloying and the carbonation keeps the alcohol from being too oppressive. You take another, long swig and roll it around in your mouth. This is actually pretty good! None of the oily or acrid flavors you’d expect from a Zaibatsu of Dhaal. This resembles the kind of cocktail you’d get at any bar across the galaxy, mixed with a little tonic water. Before you know it, you’ve finished off the whole can.

Any concern about a lack of listed side effects comes rushing to the forefront of your mind as an itching prickles across your body. You scratch your elbows, then your forearms, up to your shoulders, and across your neck. It feels like every inch of your body is blushing with a fretful irritation that just seems to get worse the more you rub it. Your antsy fidgeting grows more insistent as your touchy, tingly frame grows hot under your fingertips. You can’t take all the irritation and, finding a relatively private corner, you strip out of your equipment to scratch yourself even more vigorously.

You’re annoyed at first. What is this reaction, some kind of allergy? You only start to grow legitimately alarmed once your [pc.skinFurScalesNoun] begin"+({var0}?"":"s")+" to flake off. Every time you touch your body, the small, rigid plates lose their grip in your skin and cascade off of you in a glittering rain of discarded "+({var1}?"keratin":"scales")+". Panic subsides to comfort as you find the burning itch fading anywhere you stroke the flakes off. You may be losing your beautiful coat of [pc.skinFurScales], but the lip-biting satisfaction you get from brushing them off is too sweet to resist.
